entry.title: Re: Problems with USING ALL CLIENTS
entry.content: Hi Kevin, I think nowadays i would write that with cds-views and table-functions. In a table function you can write the sql in sqlscript (similar to abap, but has far more capapilities than regular abap in sql-expressions). In sqlscript you can use different joins and use the client-column in every-join. In sql-script you must not write "client specified" or "using all clients". Instead in the underlying cds-view you declare the client as follows, if you want to give the client-numbers dynamically: <pre> @ClientHandling.type: #CLIENT_DEPENDENT define table function zb_my_select_tf with parameters @Environment.systemField: #CLIENT i_client : vdm_v_sap_client </pre>

entry.title: Jumpstart Your HR Transformation with SAP Signavio Value Accelerators
entry.content:
The  SAP Signavio Value Accelerator Library (VAL) provides ready-to-use, SAP-aligned process content that helps organizations move faster and with less risk during transformation programs. For SAP SuccessFactors customers and partners, standard, well-structured process content is very valuable: it reduces ambiguity during fit-to-standard workshops, creates a single source of truth for process owners and implementers, and shortens the time from discovery to design and deployment. 

The value accelerator for SAP SuccessFactors can directly be downloaded from VAL. In addition to that, the SAP SuccessFactors process library (SSPL), offers the opportunity for everybody to explore the value accelerator without the prerequisite of an own workspace. Built in SAP Signavio, the SSPL is the foundation for process design and standardization in the SAP SuccessFactors domain.  

No matter whether viewed via VAL or SSPL, the SAP Signavio Value Accelerator for Process Design and Standardization for SAP SuccessFactors  delivers more than just flowcharts: it provides interactive process artifacts that expose roles, system capabilities, data elements, AI-driven activities, and recommended actions for key moments that matter in your HR transformation journey. 

AI at the Heart of Modern HR Processes 

A standout feature of this release is the comprehensive "Driven by AI" attribute, which highlights how artificial intelligence supports your HR processes across three distinct categories: 

  • Deep Learning AI: Transform talent development and employee experience with personalized recommendations, predictions, and deep insights 
  • Generative AI: Accelerate HR processes and tasks by quickly generating or summarizing text across the SAP SuccessFactors HCM suite 
  • Joule (with Navigational and Transactional use cases): Deliver a better employee experience while lowering personnel costs with a natural language processing AI copilot 

These AI capabilities are clearly marked throughout the process models, helping you identify opportunities to leverage intelligent automation in your HR operations. 

Further Key Benefits at a Glance 

  • Fast ramp-up for SAP SuccessFactors transformation programs: Jump into fit-to-standard workshops with structured, SAP-aligned models instead of starting from scratch 
  • Improved alignment between HR operations, people managers, and IT through a common process language and clearly defined handovers 
  • Process harmonization: The layered architecture and navigation maps make it easy to identify overlaps and inconsistencies across regions and business units 
  • Solution design and configuration: BPMN models include system handoffs and data artifacts that help architects identify integration needs and data mapping requirements 
  • And more: Training and adoption support, reusable, governance-ready assets, ... 

Who Should Use This Content? 

This value accelerator is designed for: 

  • HR transformation teams and business process owners 
  • SAP SuccessFactors implementation partners and consultants 
  • IT architects and solution designers 
  • Business transformation managers and process excellence teams 
  • Anyone involved in HR digitalization and standardization initiatives 

What’s Included 

As described by @Marijn in the blog “Gain a comprehensive understanding of the processes in the SAP SuccessFactors process library”, the content comes with many useful artifacts: 

End-to-End Process Views and Hierarchies 

Within the SAP Signavio Process Collaboration Hub, you can explore processes in two main ways: 

  • End-to-End Process View: Navigate the complete HR process landscape 
  • Process Hierarchy: Found under "Shared Documents" in the left-hand panel  

Three New End-to-End Journey Models 

Additionally, there are three new journey models now available in the "Process Journeys" folder: 

  1. Recruit to Onboard 
  2. Develop to Grow 
  3. Reward to Retain 

These journeys help you introduce the SAP SuccessFactors process framework in a clear, structured, and business-friendly way. Each journey provides a high-level overview of the process landscape and serves as a visual guide to the roles, applications, and subprocesses involved, perfect for establishing shared understanding across stakeholders and accelerating onboarding for teams unfamiliar with SAP Signavio or SAP SuccessFactors. 

Note: Journey Model content is only available in SAP SuccessFactors process library (SSPL), not in the content downloadable from SAP Signavio Value Accelerator Library. 

Rich Process Attributes  

Each process and activity includes detailed metadata: 

At the Process Level: 

  • Short Description, Purpose, Prerequisites, and Success Criteria 
  • Linkage to Business and Solution Capabilities 
  • AI support indicators 

Simply select a process of interest by clicking the “+” symbol and open up the panel on the right side to get the information. 

 

At the Activity Level: 

  • Fuchsia desktop icon: Indicates the SAP SuccessFactors capability used 
  • Orange person icon: Marks a "Moment that Matter" 
  • Cyan cogwheel icon: Denotes support by SAP Business AI 

Overlays highlight these attributes, while details can be explored by clicking on an activity which opens up the panel on the right side. 

Process Roles: 
Each swim lane represents a responsible role. Selecting a swim lane reveals: 

  • Role Description 
  • Process Role Group (e.g., HR or Business) 
  • Process Role Category: 
    • Business: "Manager" or "Individual Contributor" 
    • HR: Based on Dave Ulrich’s 3-pillar model: "HR Consultancy," "HR Expertise," "HR Operations," plus "HR IT" 

Data Elements: 
Activities may be linked to input/output documents. When selecting a document, the side panel displays - in addition to the description: 

  • Data Type (SuccessFactors, 3rd party, or non-system data) 
  • Data Element (for SAP SuccessFactors data) 
  • Data Element Grouping (e.g., EC data grouped under Personal or Employment Information) 

 

Downloadable Process Summaries 

Based on customer feedback, we’ve added one-pager process summaries containing: 

  • Key metadata for each process 
  • Direct link to the respective process in the SAP Signavio Collaboration Hub 

How to Access the Content 

Getting started is simple: 

  • Explore in SAP SuccessFactors process library (SSPL): Visit the SAP SuccessFactors Process Library in Signavio to explore the content interactively without needing your own workspace 
  • Download from the Value Accelerator Library (VAL): Download the complete package directly from VAL for use in your own SAP Signavio workspace.  

  • Watch the demo: Check out  this video for a complete walkthrough on how to explore the content in SSPL and download it from VAL

 

For a first overview, we encourage you to explore the journeys in the SAP SuccessFactors process library and use them as a starting point when introducing processes to your stakeholders. Additionally, you can leverage the process summaries for quick reference and stakeholder communication. 

You can look into these resources should you require further information on how to work with the SAP Signavio Value Accelerator Library:  

  • L1 Deck with overview of SAP Signavio Value Accelerators and their benefits. 
  • User guide for VAL on SAP Help. 
  • For more information on the SAP SuccessFactors process library, please refer to the SAP SuccessFactors Community. 

A big thank you to everyone who contributed to this release! Special kudos to the authors of the SAP SuccessFactors process content for their detailed modeling and practical guidance. Share your feedback with your HCM Business Partner or send us an email to SSPL@sap.com  to help us refine and improve these resources to drive future enhancements 

Ready to Transform Your HR Processes? 

Explore the SAP Signavio Value Accelerator for process design and standardization for SAP SuccessFactors today and turn process complexity into competitive advantage!

entry.title: Re: Variable Pay - history Assigment data
entry.content: I have a couple of thoughts. 1. WHY???, what are they trying to accomplish by determining the BU that includes the most days worked? I would get an understanding of the requirement and determine if this is really necessary. 2. How many BU's do they have? If it is a manageable number, you can use conditional logic to say something like: if(BU=BU1, # of days, else 0) at the assignment level then aggregate at the entry level to determine which column is the greatest. Once they have determined the BU, what are they going to do with the information? You should understand the end game before trying to build anything because it may be unnecessary work.

entry.title: Configuring Singapore Localization in Onboarding (ONB): Mapping NRIC and CPF Fields
entry.content:
Hi All,

Hi everyone,

I am working on a SuccessFactors Onboarding implementation for a Singapore-based entity. I am looking for guidance on the standard best practices for Singapore localization, specifically:

  1. Personal Data Collection: What is the recommended way to configure the validation for NRIC/FIN numbers during the onboarding process to ensure they follow the standard SG format?

  2. Compliance Forms: Are there standard MDF objects or pre-delivered Document Templates for Singapore-specific forms like the Form P1 or bank account collection (including Bank/Branch codes)?

  3. Data Mapping: Are there specific "gotchas" when mapping these localized fields from Recruiting (RCM) to Onboarding and finally to Employee Central (EC)?

Any documentation or Implementation Design Principles (IDPs) specific to Singapore would be greatly appreciated!

entry.title: Re: Empty Leg optimization using TM Optimizer
entry.content:
Hi Tom,

I came across your example where you model an empty leg followed by a loaded leg, and I found it very relevant to a scenario I am currently working on. I assume your solution is based on an on‑premise or private cloud TM setup.

I am trying to apply a comparable concept in SAP S/4HANA Public Cloud TM, specifically to cover the following scenario:

  • an empty leg (A → B) for collection
  • followed by a loaded leg (B → A) for transportation

So far, I have not found a standard way to model this cleanly in the public cloud scope. From your experience, do you see any viable workaround or recommended approach in TM Public Cloud to handle this type of empty-leg planning, or are there functional limitations that would prevent this altogether?

Any insight would be highly appreciated.

Best regards,

Tomas

entry.title: Alphanumeric CNPJ: What You Need to Know
entry.content:
Brazil’s tax reform introduces several structural changes across the fiscal landscape, and one of the most impactful is the adoption of the alphanumeric CNPJ. As this new identifier takes shape, SAP systems must be ready.

This blog provides an overview of the change, explains why it is being introduced, outlines the SAP areas affected, and highlights the delivery timelines and key references you should be aware of to prepare for compliance.

What Is Changing?

The Brazilian CNPJ (National Register of Legal Entities) has always been a 14-digit numeric identifier. Starting in July 2026, it will expand to include both letters and numbers, becoming an alphanumeric identifier.

This change aims to:

  • Increase the available combination space for taxpayer identification
  • Improve the classification of entities, branches, and establishments
  • Provide more flexibility and scalability for the new tax model

SAP's Preparation and Timeline

SAP is actively updating its solutions to accommodate the new format. The central reference is SAP Note 3714871 – Tax Reform: Alphanumeric CNPJ Leading SAP Note, which consolidates guidance, impacted objects, delivery plans, and related documentation.

This is the primary source you should follow.

Delivery plans can be accessed through the following links:

  • Tax Reform - Alphanumeric CNPJ - Normative Instruction No. 2229, of October 15,
  • Tax Reform - Joint Technical Note 2025.001 - Alphanumeric CNPJ

Key Areas Affected

SAP has identified several modules and processes affected by the new CNPJ format. Key areas include:

  • Business Partner: Fields now support alphanumeric input with updated validation rules and error messages
    • Adjustment delivered in SAP Note 3583627
  • Logistics and Nota Fiscal Database: Structural updates to tables, NF-e repository, integration frameworks and tax transactions
  • Document and Reporting Compliance (NF-e): Updates to electronic nota fiscal, digital signature, and schema validations
  • Human Capital Management (HCM) Brazil (Payroll): Adaptation of eSocial solution (layouts, event tables, and extraction process), employer records, employee master data and HCM reports
  • Finance and Banking: Changes to CNAB files, PIX, TED, and Boleto payment methods, updates in routines for generating payment files

SAP System Changes in Customizing (IMG)

Adjustments already delivered within the SAP Customizing environment include:

Global Company Parameters

SPRO → Financial Accounting → Financial Accounting Global Settings → Global Parameters → Update Additional Parameters

Business Place Definition

SPRO → Cross-Application Components → General Application Functions → Business Place → Define Business Places

These areas now fully support alphanumeric CNPJ values.

ABAP Objects Impacted

SAP Note 3714871 provides a detailed list of ABAP objects expected to be modified. These objects are being converted from numeric to character-based (CHAR) fields to support the new identifier.

SAP recommends that you review custom developments (Z objects) and integrations that reference numeric CNPJ fields, as these may need adjustments.

This includes:

  • Tables
  • Data elements
  • Domain definitions
  • Interfaces
  • Business Add-In (BAdI) implementations
  • Enhancements
  • Custom reports

Planned Delivery Timeline

Important: The expected delivery dates are subject to change without prior notice and represent only an estimate provided by SAP, not a contractual commitment for delivery.

HCM Brazil (Payroll)

  • Phase 1: March 13, 2026
  • Phase 2: March 31, 2026

Logistics and Nota Fiscal Database Adjustments

  • Planned delivery: April 17, 2026 (Prerequisite for all other Logistics, Document and Reporting Compliance, and Financial updates)

Document and Reporting Compliance (NF-e) – Public Cloud and Private Cloud/On-Premise

  • Planned delivery: April 17, 2026

Business Partner and Statutory Reports

  • Planned delivery: April 17, 2026

Banking Files – CNAB / Payment Methods

  • CNAB Private Cloud/On-Premise: April 16, 2026FICA Private Cloud/On-Premise (PIX, TED, Boleto included): April 16, 2026

Key References

SAP Note

  • 3714871 – Tax Reform: Alphanumeric CNPJ Leading SAP Note

Regulatory Change Manager Links

  • HCM Impacts – Phase 1 and Phase 2
  • Nota Fiscal Database Adjustments
  • Business Partner and Statutory Reports
  • CNAB Files
  • CNAB Files FICA

Note: The links provided is specific to the Product Impact of S/4HANA Private Cloud. For other products, such as S/4HANA Private Cloud/on‑premise, SAP ERP, SAP S/4 Public Cloud, refer to the regulatory change manager links related to each product.

What Should You Do Now?

Start by reviewing SAP Note 3714871 and evaluating any custom developments that reference CNPJ fields. Early preparation will help minimize disruption and ensure your organization stays compliant as Brazil's tax infrastructure evolves.

In summary, your action plan should be:

  1. Review SAP Note 3714871 in detail
  2. Evaluate all custom developments that reference CNPJ fields
  3. Track delivery updates via the regulatory change manager
  4. Plan for early testing in sandbox environment
  5. Update internal processes and integrations (banks, partners, NF-e service providers, tax engines, etc.)

Early preparation will help you avoid disruption and maintain compliance as Brazil's tax landscape evolves. The clock is ticking, but with the right approach, you can navigate this transition smoothly.

For more information or if you have any questions about the Brazilian tax reform,  visit the dedicated Tax Reform Work Zone — your hub for updates, resources, and deeper insights.

Did you enjoy this post? Give it a Like and share it with your colleagues! Feel free to leave your feedback or comments in the space below. And remember to follow the SAP S/4HANA Logistics for Brazil tag in the SAP Community to stay tuned for the latest news. 

 
Thanks for reading — until next time!
Luize

entry.title: Re: CDS: how to use EXTEND VIEW.
entry.content:
Hello guys,

Regarding SAP S/4HANA Public Cloud system, 

I need to create an extensible field for the work center business context, but this business object isn't extensible. Does anyone know if there's a way to add the field (read-only) and also include it in the standard API API_WORK_CENTER?

https://api.sap.com/bointerface/I_WORKCENTERTP

https://api.sap.com/api/WORKCENTER_0001/overview

Thank you.

entry.title: Production Agentic SAP Systems: Monitoring, Cost, Failure, and Scale (Series 2, Part 6 - complete)
entry.content:
SAP COMMUNITY  |  SERIES 2: AGENTIC AI IN PRACTICE  |  PART 6 OF 6  |  SERIES COMPLETE

Production Agentic SAP Systems: Monitoring, Cost, Failure, and Scale

The final part of this series covers what happens after deployment — monitoring agent health, controlling token costs, handling failures gracefully, tracing execution end to end with LangSmith and HANA Cloud, scaling across multiple SAP landscapes, and managing agent versioning and lifecycle in production.

By Deepa Dorairaj   |  SAP AI Solution Architect  |  Series 2, Part 6 of 6  |  Published 2026

TL;DR: Deploying an agentic SAP system is not the finish line. It is the starting line for a different set of problems — monitoring agent health, controlling model spend, handling failures without losing state, tracing what the agent actually did, scaling across multiple landscapes, and managing agent versions across environments. This article covers all six production concerns with working code patterns, using SAP migration as the primary context while keeping every pattern applicable to any agentic SAP workload.

Series Context

This is Part 6 — the final part — of the Agentic AI in Practice series. Parts 1–5 established agentic architecture, ReAct agents, multi-agent orchestration, migration-specific agents, and human-in-the-loop patterns. This part covers what sustains those systems in production — the operational layer that determines whether an agentic SAP deployment lasts six months or six years.

The patterns here apply universally. Whether your agent handles migration reconciliation (Part 4), S/4HANA change request approvals (Part 5), or any other SAP workflow — the production operational concerns are the same.

TOPIC 1: MONITORING AGENT HEALTH AND PERFORMANCE 

  ▶  Health Monitoring — Know When Your Agent Is Struggling Before Users Do 

Monitoring Agent Health in Production

Agent health in production goes beyond application uptime. An agent can be running without errors while performing poorly — taking too long, consuming too many tokens, producing low-confidence outputs, or routing to human escalation at unexpectedly high rates. Health monitoring must capture all of these dimensions.

Metric

Definition

Target

Alert Trigger

Response latency

Time from agent invocation to final output

< 30s for interactive, < 5min for batch

Alert if p95 exceeds threshold

Token consumption per run

Total tokens used across all LLM calls in one agent execution

Baseline × 1.5 max

Spike indicates prompt bloat or loop

Escalation rate

% of runs routed to human review

< 20% for mature agents

Rising rate signals degrading confidence

Autonomous success rate

% of runs completed without human intervention

> 80% for mature agents

Falling rate signals model or data drift

Node failure rate

% of individual LangGraph nodes that throw exceptions

< 1%

Persistent node failures need investigation

# Agent health monitoring — write metrics to HANA Cloud after each run
from dataclasses import dataclass
from datetime import datetime
import time
 
@dataclass
class AgentRunMetrics:
    run_id:              str
    agent_name:          str
    agent_version:       str
    start_time:          str
    end_time:            str
    duration_seconds:    float
    total_tokens:        int
    prompt_tokens:       int
    completion_tokens:   int
    llm_calls:           int
    nodes_executed:      int
    nodes_failed:        int
    escalated:           bool
    outcome:             str    # SUCCESS | ESCALATED | FAILED | TIMEOUT
    error_message:       str
 
class AgentHealthMonitor:
    def __init__(self, hana_conn):
        self.conn = hana_conn
        self._ensure_metrics_table()
 
    def _ensure_metrics_table(self):
        self.conn.cursor().execute('''
            CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS AI_OPS.AGENT_RUN_METRICS (
                RUN_ID           NVARCHAR(100) PRIMARY KEY,
                AGENT_NAME       NVARCHAR(100),
                AGENT_VERSION    NVARCHAR(20),
                START_TIME       TIMESTAMP,
                END_TIME         TIMESTAMP,
                DURATION_SECONDS DECIMAL(10,2),
                TOTAL_TOKENS     INTEGER,
                PROMPT_TOKENS    INTEGER,
                COMPLETION_TOKENS INTEGER,
                LLM_CALLS        INTEGER,
                NODES_EXECUTED   INTEGER,
                NODES_FAILED     INTEGER,
                ESCALATED        BOOLEAN,
                OUTCOME          NVARCHAR(20),
                ERROR_MESSAGE    NCLOB
            )''')
        self.conn.commit()
 
    def record_run(self, metrics: AgentRunMetrics):
        cursor = self.conn.cursor()
        cursor.execute('''
            INSERT INTO AI_OPS.AGENT_RUN_METRICS VALUES
            (?,?,?,?,?,?,?,?,?,?,?,?,?,?,?)''',
            [metrics.run_id, metrics.agent_name, metrics.agent_version,
             metrics.start_time, metrics.end_time, metrics.duration_seconds,
             metrics.total_tokens, metrics.prompt_tokens, metrics.completion_tokens,
             metrics.llm_calls, metrics.nodes_executed, metrics.nodes_failed,
             metrics.escalated, metrics.outcome, metrics.error_message])
        self.conn.commit()
 
    def get_health_summary(self, agent_name: str, hours: int = 24) -> dict:
        """Daily health summary — escalation rate, avg duration, failure rate."""
        cursor = self.conn.cursor()
        cursor.execute('''
            SELECT
                COUNT(*) as total_runs,
                AVG(DURATION_SECONDS) as avg_duration,
                SUM(TOTAL_TOKENS) as total_tokens,
                SUM(CASE WHEN ESCALATED THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) as escalations,
                SUM(CASE WHEN OUTCOME = 'FAILED' THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) as failures
            FROM AI_OPS.AGENT_RUN_METRICS
            WHERE AGENT_NAME = ?
            AND START_TIME >= ADD_SECONDS(NOW(), ?)
        ''', [agent_name, -(hours * 3600)])
        row = cursor.fetchone()
        total = row[0] or 1
        return {
            'total_runs': row[0], 'avg_duration_sec': round(row[1] or 0, 2),
            'total_tokens': row[2] or 0,
            'escalation_rate_pct': round((row[3] or 0) / total * 100, 1),
            'failure_rate_pct': round((row[4] or 0) / total * 100, 1)
        }

TOPIC 2: COST MANAGEMENT — TOKEN USAGE AND MODEL SPEND 

  ▶  Cost Controls — Agent Spend That Doesn't Surprise You at Month End 

Controlling Token Costs in Production

Foundation model costs in production agentic systems are easy to underestimate during development. A prompt that costs $0.002 per call is trivial in testing. When an agent runs 500 times per day across a migration project, it becomes a material line item. Cost management is not optional — it is an architecture decision made before deployment.

Track tokens per node → Set budget per agent run → Alert on overrun → Enforce hard limits → Report weekly to leadership
# Cost management — token budget enforcement per agent run
class TokenBudgetManager:
    """
    Enforces token budgets per agent run.
    Tracks spend against configurable limits.
    Raises BudgetExceededError before hard limit is hit.
    """
 
    # Model cost per 1M tokens (input / output) — update as pricing changes
    MODEL_COSTS = {
        'gpt-4o':                  {'input': 2.50,  'output': 10.00},
        'gpt-4o-mini':             {'input': 0.15,  'output': 0.60},
        'claude-sonnet-4-20250514': {'input': 3.00,  'output': 15.00},
        'claude-haiku-4-5':         {'input': 0.80,  'output': 4.00},
    }
 
    def __init__(self, soft_limit_usd: float, hard_limit_usd: float):
        self.soft_limit = soft_limit_usd
        self.hard_limit = hard_limit_usd
        self.total_spend = 0.0
        self.token_log = []
 
    def record_llm_call(self, model: str, prompt_tokens: int, completion_tokens: int):
        costs = self.MODEL_COSTS.get(model, {'input': 5.0, 'output': 15.0})
        call_cost = (prompt_tokens * costs['input'] +
                     completion_tokens * costs['output']) / 1_000_000
        self.total_spend += call_cost
        self.token_log.append({
            'model': model, 'prompt_tokens': prompt_tokens,
            'completion_tokens': completion_tokens, 'cost_usd': call_cost
        })
 
        if self.total_spend >= self.hard_limit:
            raise BudgetExceededError(
                f'Hard limit ${self.hard_limit} exceeded. Spent: ${self.total_spend:.4f}')
 
        if self.total_spend >= self.soft_limit:
            # Log warning — agent continues but ops team is alerted
            log_cost_alert(self.total_spend, self.soft_limit)
 
    def get_cost_summary(self) -> dict:
        return {
            'total_spend_usd': round(self.total_spend, 6),
            'total_calls': len(self.token_log),
            'total_tokens': sum(t['prompt_tokens'] + t['completion_tokens']
                               for t in self.token_log),
            'by_model': {}  # Group token_log by model for breakdown
        }

Set hard limits conservatively during the first 30 days of production. Agent behavior under real workloads often differs from testing — prompt lengths, token counts, and LLM call frequency can all be higher than expected. Start with tight limits and raise them based on observed spend patterns rather than setting generous limits upfront.

TOPIC 3: FAILURE HANDLING AND RETRY LOGIC 

  ▶  Failure Handling — Agents That Fail Gracefully Rather Than Silently 

Failure Handling and Retry Logic

LangGraph agents can fail at multiple levels — individual LLM calls, tool executions, SAP API calls, or entire node functions. Each failure type requires a different response. Transient failures (rate limits, network timeouts) should retry. Deterministic failures (invalid inputs, missing permissions) should fail fast with context. State corruption should trigger safe halt.

 

Failure Type

Category

Response

Action

LLM rate limit (429)

Transient

Exponential backoff retry — 3 attempts max

Log each retry attempt

LLM timeout

Transient

Retry once with extended timeout, then fallback model

Alert if fallback used

SAP API 401/403

Deterministic

Fail fast — token expired or permissions issue

Trigger token refresh or escalate

SAP API 404

Deterministic

Fail fast — object not found, log with context

No retry — surface to human

JSON parse error from LLM

Deterministic

Retry prompt with stricter JSON instruction once

If retry fails, escalate

State corruption / unexpected type

Critical

Safe halt — preserve last known good state

Immediate alert to ops team

# Retry decorator with exponential backoff for transient failures
import functools, time, random
from typing import Type, Tuple
 
def retry_with_backoff(
    max_attempts: int = 3,
    base_delay: float = 1.0,
    max_delay: float = 60.0,
    retryable_exceptions: Tuple[Type[Exception], ...] = (Exception,)
):
    def decorator(func):
        @functools.wraps(func)
        def wrapper(*args, **kwargs):
            last_exception = None
            for attempt in range(max_attempts):
                try:
                    return func(*args, **kwargs)
                except retryable_exceptions as e:
                    last_exception = e
                    if attempt == max_attempts - 1:
                        raise
                    # Exponential backoff with jitter
                    delay = min(base_delay * (2 ** attempt) + random.uniform(0, 1),
                                max_delay)
                    print(f'Attempt {attempt + 1} failed: {e}. Retrying in {delay:.1f}s')
                    time.sleep(delay)
            raise last_exception
        return wrapper
    return decorator
 
# Apply to LLM calls in agent nodes
@retry_with_backoff(max_attempts=3, base_delay=2.0,
                    retryable_exceptions=(RateLimitError, TimeoutError))
def call_llm_with_retry(llm, messages):
    return llm(messages)
 
# Fallback model pattern — switch to cheaper model if primary fails
def call_with_fallback(primary_llm, fallback_llm, messages, budget_manager):
    try:
        response = call_llm_with_retry(primary_llm, messages)
        budget_manager.record_llm_call('gpt-4o', ...)
        return response
    except Exception as e:
        print(f'Primary model failed: {e}. Using fallback.')
        response = fallback_llm(messages)
        budget_manager.record_llm_call('gpt-4o-mini', ...)
        return response

TOPIC 4: OBSERVABILITY — TRACING AGENT EXECUTION END TO END 

  ▶  Observability — See Exactly What Your Agent Did and Why 

End-to-End Observability with LangSmith, HANA Cloud, and SAP Cloud Logging

Observability for agentic systems is different from application logging. You need to trace not just what happened but the reasoning chain — what the agent was thinking at each step, which tools it called, what the inputs and outputs were, and where it deviated from expected behavior. Three layers provide complete coverage:

  • LangSmith — detailed LLM-level tracing, token counts, latency per call, prompt and response capture
  • HANA Cloud audit tables — business-level decision trail from Part 5, durable and queryable for compliance
  • SAP Cloud Logging — application-level logs for infrastructure events, errors, and system health

LangSmith Integration

# Enable LangSmith tracing — set environment variables before agent runs
import os
os.environ['LANGCHAIN_TRACING_V2'] = 'true'
os.environ['LANGCHAIN_ENDPOINT'] = 'https://api.smith.langchain.com'
os.environ['LANGCHAIN_API_KEY'] = 'your_langsmith_api_key'
os.environ['LANGCHAIN_PROJECT'] = 'sap-migration-agents'
 
# All LangChain/LangGraph calls are now automatically traced
# View traces at https://smith.langchain.com — filter by project
# Each trace shows: node execution order, LLM inputs/outputs,
# token counts, latency per step, and full reasoning chain

SAP Cloud Logging Integration

# SAP Cloud Logging — structured JSON logs for infrastructure events
import logging, json
 
class SAPCloudLogger:
    """
    Structured logger compatible with SAP Cloud Logging service.
    On BTP CF, logs written to stdout are automatically captured
    by SAP Cloud Logging if the service is bound to the application.
    """
    def __init__(self, agent_name: str, version: str):
        self.agent_name = agent_name
        self.version = version
        logging.basicConfig(level=logging.INFO,
                            format='%(message)s')  # JSON output
 
    def log(self, level: str, event: str, **kwargs):
        entry = {
            'timestamp': datetime.now().isoformat(),
            'level': level,
            'agent': self.agent_name,
            'version': self.version,
            'event': event,
            **kwargs
        }
        logging.info(json.dumps(entry))
 
    def info(self, event: str, **kwargs):  self.log('INFO', event, **kwargs)
    def warn(self, event: str, **kwargs):  self.log('WARN', event, **kwargs)
    def error(self, event: str, **kwargs): self.log('ERROR', event, **kwargs)

The three observability layers serve different audiences. LangSmith is for developers debugging agent behavior. HANA Cloud audit tables are for compliance and business stakeholders. SAP Cloud Logging is for infrastructure and operations teams. Deploy all three from day one — retrofitting observability after production incidents is significantly more expensive than building it in.

TOPIC 5: SCALING ACROSS MULTIPLE SAP LANDSCAPES 

  ▶  Scaling — One Agent Codebase Across Multiple SAP Environments 

Scaling Agentic Workloads Across Multiple SAP Landscapes

Enterprise SAP landscapes are not single systems. A typical organization runs development, quality, and production environments — often across multiple regions or business units. Agentic systems that work in one landscape must be deployable across all of them without code changes, relying only on environment-specific configuration.

# Multi-landscape configuration — environment-driven, no code changes
import os
 
class LandscapeConfig:
    """
    Loads landscape-specific configuration from environment variables.
    The same agent code runs across DEV, QA, and PROD
    by changing only the environment variables at deployment time.
    """
 
    def __init__(self):
        self.landscape = os.environ.get('SAP_LANDSCAPE', 'DEV')
 
        # SAP system connections
        self.source_odata_url = os.environ['SOURCE_ODATA_URL']
        self.target_odata_url = os.environ['TARGET_ODATA_URL']
        self.hana_host        = os.environ['HANA_HOST']
 
        # AI Core — same instance across landscapes, different resource groups
        self.ai_api_url        = os.environ['AICORE_BASE_URL']
        self.resource_group    = os.environ.get('AICORE_RESOURCE_GROUP', 'default')
 
        # Agent behavior — tune per landscape
        self.max_concurrent_agents = int(os.environ.get('MAX_CONCURRENT_AGENTS', '3'))
        self.token_budget_usd      = float(os.environ.get('TOKEN_BUDGET_USD', '5.0'))
        self.escalation_days       = int(os.environ.get('ESCALATION_DAYS', '7'))
 
        # Model selection per landscape — cheaper models for DEV/QA
        self.primary_model = {
            'DEV':  'gpt-4o-mini',
            'QA':   'gpt-4o-mini',
            'PROD': 'gpt-4o'
        }.get(self.landscape, 'gpt-4o-mini')

BTP CF Multi-Environment Manifest

# manifest.yml — deploy same app to DEV, QA, PROD with different env vars
applications:
  - name: sap-migration-agent-dev
    env:
      SAP_LANDSCAPE:          DEV
      MAX_CONCURRENT_AGENTS:  2
      TOKEN_BUDGET_USD:        1.0
      AICORE_RESOURCE_GROUP:  dev-agents
 
  - name: sap-migration-agent-prod
    env:
      SAP_LANDSCAPE:          PROD
      MAX_CONCURRENT_AGENTS:  10
      TOKEN_BUDGET_USD:        50.0
      AICORE_RESOURCE_GROUP:  prod-agents

Use separate AI Core resource groups per landscape (dev-agents, qa-agents, prod-agents). This isolates model deployments, resource consumption, and audit logs across environments. Sharing a resource group between DEV and PROD means a DEV agent run appears in PROD audit logs and consumes PROD token budgets.

TOPIC 6: AGENT VERSIONING AND LIFECYCLE MANAGEMENT 

  ▶  Agent Versioning — Deploy, Roll Back, and Retire Agents Safely 

Agent Versioning and Lifecycle Management

Agents are not static applications. Prompts are refined, models are updated, new tools are added, and business rules change. Without versioning, a prompt improvement in development can break production behavior silently — there is no diff, no rollback path, and no way to correlate a performance change with a specific deployment.

# Agent version registry — track what is deployed where
from dataclasses import dataclass
from datetime import datetime
 
@dataclass
class AgentVersion:
    agent_name:      str
    version:         str    # Semantic version e.g. '2.1.0'
    landscape:       str    # DEV | QA | PROD
    deployed_at:     str
    deployed_by:     str
    model_name:      str    # Foundation model used
    prompt_hash:     str    # SHA256 of system prompts — detects silent prompt changes
    status:          str    # ACTIVE | DEPRECATED | RETIRED
    change_summary:  str    # What changed in this version
 
class AgentVersionRegistry:
    def __init__(self, hana_conn):
        self.conn = hana_conn
        self._ensure_registry_table()
 
    def register_deployment(self, version: AgentVersion):
        """Record a new agent deployment — deprecate previous active version."""
        cursor = self.conn.cursor()
 
        # Deprecate current active version for this agent + landscape
        cursor.execute('''
            UPDATE AI_OPS.AGENT_VERSIONS SET STATUS = 'DEPRECATED'
            WHERE AGENT_NAME = ? AND LANDSCAPE = ? AND STATUS = 'ACTIVE'
        ''', [version.agent_name, version.landscape])
 
        # Register new version as active
        cursor.execute('''
            INSERT INTO AI_OPS.AGENT_VERSIONS VALUES (?,?,?,?,?,?,?,?,?)
        ''', [version.agent_name, version.version, version.landscape,
              version.deployed_at, version.deployed_by, version.model_name,
              version.prompt_hash, 'ACTIVE', version.change_summary])
        self.conn.commit()
 
    def rollback(self, agent_name: str, landscape: str, to_version: str):
        """Roll back to a previously deployed version."""
        cursor = self.conn.cursor()
        cursor.execute('''
            UPDATE AI_OPS.AGENT_VERSIONS SET STATUS = 'DEPRECATED'
            WHERE AGENT_NAME = ? AND LANDSCAPE = ? AND STATUS = 'ACTIVE'
        ''', [agent_name, landscape])
        cursor.execute('''
            UPDATE AI_OPS.AGENT_VERSIONS SET STATUS = 'ACTIVE'
            WHERE AGENT_NAME = ? AND LANDSCAPE = ? AND VERSION = ?
        ''', [agent_name, landscape, to_version])
        self.conn.commit()
        print(f'Rolled back {agent_name} in {landscape} to version {to_version}')

Prompt Hashing — Detect Silent Prompt Changes

# Hash system prompts to detect unintended changes between deployments
import hashlib
 
def get_prompt_hash(system_prompts: dict) -> str:
    """
    SHA256 hash of all system prompts concatenated.
    If the hash changes between versions, prompts changed.
    Store in version registry to detect accidental prompt drift.
    """
    prompt_str = '|'.join(f'{k}:{v}' for k, v in sorted(system_prompts.items()))
    return hashlib.sha256(prompt_str.encode()).hexdigest()[:16]

Prompt hashing is one of the most underused practices in production agentic systems. Agent behavior is determined more by prompts than by code — a small prompt change can dramatically alter output quality and decision patterns. Tracking prompt hashes in the version registry means you can correlate any behavioral change with exactly which deployment introduced it.

PRODUCTION READINESS CHECKLIST 

Before You Go Live: Production Readiness Checklist

 

Checklist Item

Verification

Health monitoring

AGENT_RUN_METRICS table deployed, baseline metrics captured from staging

Cost controls

Token budget limits set, soft and hard limits configured, cost alerts enabled

Failure handling

Retry logic on all LLM calls, fallback model configured, safe halt on state corruption

LangSmith tracing

LANGCHAIN_TRACING_V2 enabled, project name set, traces verified in LangSmith UI

HANA Cloud audit trail

AI_AUDIT.AGENT_DECISIONS table deployed, all human-in-the-loop nodes logging

SAP Cloud Logging

Structured JSON logs configured, Cloud Logging service bound to BTP CF app

Multi-landscape config

All sensitive values in environment variables, no hardcoded credentials

Separate resource groups

DEV, QA, PROD resource groups created in AI Core, isolated from each other

Version registry

AI_OPS.AGENT_VERSIONS table deployed, initial version registered at deployment

Rollback plan

Previous version deployable within 15 minutes, rollback procedure documented

Escalation contacts

ESCALATION_MATRIX configured with real email addresses per exception type

Approval SLAs defined

Timeout values set per approval type, fallback strategies configured

Series Complete — What You've Built Across Six Parts

Across six articles, this series built a complete foundation for agentic AI in SAP:

  • Part 1: Agentic AI concepts and your first LangGraph agent on SAP AI Core
  • Part 2: ReAct agents with SAP tools — dynamic reasoning and tool selection
  • Part 3: Multi-agent orchestration — coordinating specialized SAP agents
  • Part 4: Five migration agents — assessment, validation, runbooks, monitoring, reconciliation
  • Part 5: Human-in-the-loop patterns — approval, escalation, audit trail
  • Part 6: Production operations — health, cost, failure, observability, scaling, versioning

The architecture that connects all six parts is not theoretical. Every pattern here has been applied to real SAP workstreams — ECC to S/4HANA migration, HANA Cloud transformation, DMLT workstreams, change request management. The code is production-ready with appropriate caveats for your specific landscape configuration.

If you've followed this series and built something — share it in the comments. The community variations are where the most valuable refinements emerge. And if you're just starting, Part 1 is the entry point. The full series builds progressively — each part assumes the previous one.

entry.title: Re: Our 2026 Roadmap for Joule for Developers ABAP AI capabilities
entry.content:
Hi Signortintin, 

a wise man once said "Content is king". For AI I would rephrase this to "Context is king". You have to remember that without context all AI models are just statistics engines which propose by probability based on decades of legacy ABAP code.

For sure data elements are not variables and using a variable prefix for a type definition creates confusion. Also all modern ABAP Style Guides advise against "Hungarian Notation" (technical prefixes) because they often encode information already known to the compiler, making code harder to read. 

So why do LLMs still sometimes propose that? Because "Context is king" and if it is lacking or the context window is just too big it takes the most probable based on statistics (which is based on decades of legacy ABAP code). LLMs often suggest names like LV_VALUE because they are trained on a massive volume of legacy code and simplified tutorials. For decades, many ABAP developers used "Hungarian Notation" (technical prefixes) religiously. Public repositories, old SAP documentation, and forum posts (like this one now as well) are filled with such examples.

You can fix this by adding a line to your prompt like: "Follow Clean ABAP naming conventions and use business-semantic names for Dictionary objects." In addition models perform significantly better when they see a "Before vs. After", comparison or guidance like "Do not use technical prefixes like..."

Cheers

Jan

entry.title: Problem with Fiori outboundNavigation when Personas is active
entry.content:
Hello experts, 

we have a Fiori App with Fiori Elements and Odata 4. We use Personas. We have some actions in fronted to navigate to an external URL. The Navigation works properly. But only the first time into the Launchpad, when you click the Button does'n happens, only from the second time.

entry.title: Sales Order Discount and Free Goods Scheme – Public Cloud Feasibility
entry.content:
Hello Community,

Need your support and thoughts for the below requirement On Public Cloud

Sales Order Pricing & Free Goods Requirement

  1. Discount Logic (Quantity 1–9):
    For sales orders with a total quantity between 1 and 9 (across multiple materials (Products)/SKUs), a 5% discount should be applied automatically using condition type ZD01 in the pricing procedure.

 

  1. Free Goods Logic (Quantity-Based Slabs):
  • For order quantities between 10 and 19 (across different SKUs/Products), 1 free good item should be automatically added.
  • If the primary free good is not available in stock, the system should automatically check and allocate the next available substitute material.
  • For order quantities between 20 and 29, 2 free goods should be applied.
  • If the required free goods are not available, the system should similarly check alternative substitutes and allocate them automatically in the sales order.
  • This logic should be applicable for all defined quantity slabs, with automatic substitution based on stock availability.
  1. Scheme Maintenance (Excel Upload):
    An Excel upload functionality should be provided to maintain schemes and free goods data.
    Since these schemes are subject to frequent (weekly) changes, the solution should allow easy updates through bulk upload.

Regards,

Ashish

entry.title: Updated Instructor-Led Trainings: SAP Commerce Cloud Developer – Part 1 & Part 2
entry.content:
We are happy to share that the Instructor-Led Trainings (ILTs) for SAP Commerce Cloud Developer – Part 1 and Part 2 have been updated! These updates ensure our developer enablement remains aligned with the latest SAP Commerce Cloud direction, current best practices, and real-world project needs.

Whether you are new to SAP Commerce Cloud or already working on implementation projects, these refreshed courses provide a solid, hands-on learning experience.

🧩 SAP Commerce Cloud Developer – Part 1 (C4H340)

SAP Commerce Cloud Developer – Part 1 is designed to build a strong foundation for developers getting started with the platform.

What this course is about:

  • SAP Commerce Cloud architecture and extension-based development
  • Core concepts such as items, types, and relations
  • Platform configuration and customization
  • Administration and backoffice tooling
  •  Introduction to data modeling and development basics

By the end of the course, participants understand how the platform works, how extensions interact, and how to implement basic custom development in a structured and upgrade-safe way.

Training Shop link:
SAP Commerce Cloud Developer Part 1 

🚀SAP Commerce Cloud Developer – Part 2 (C4H341)

SAP Commerce Cloud Developer – Part 2 is the natural continuation of Part 1 and focuses on advanced development topics commonly used in customer projects.

What this course is about:

  • Advanced customization of backoffice and platform features
  • SAP Commerce Web Services (OCC) and integrations
  • Search, personalization, promotions, and coupons
  • CronJobs, events, and the process engine
  • Extensions for order management and fulfillment

After completing Part 2, participants are better prepared to design, extend, and maintain SAP Commerce Cloud solutions used in complex, enterprise-scale scenarios.

Training Shop link:
SAP Commerce Cloud Developer Part 2 

🆕What’s New in the Updated Trainings?

The updated SAP Commerce Cloud Developer – Part 1 and Part 2 courses focus on improving clarity, relevance, and hands-on value.

Key highlights:

  • Content aligned with recent SAP Commerce Cloud releases
  • Updated hands-on exercises reflecting real project scenarios
  • Improved development workflows following best practices
  • Clearer explanations of complex technical topics

These improvements help developers better understand not only how to build on SAP Commerce Cloud, but also why certain approaches are recommended.

🖥️ Learning System Access (LSA): Hands-On Practice Included

The current update includes enhancements to the Learning System Access (LSA), enabling participants to engage in self-paced learning within a dedicated SAP Commerce Cloud training environment.

With LSA, learners can:

  • Work in a preconfigured SAP Commerce Cloud system
  • Perform hands-on exercises in a safe environment
  • Experiment and troubleshoot without risk to productive systems
  • Reinforce learning through practical application

This hands-on access plays a crucial role in turning theoretical knowledge into practical development skills.

🎯Why Join These Trainings?

  • Instructor-led sessions with SAP-authorized trainers
  • A strong mix of theory and hands-on exercises
  • Up-to-date content aligned with SAP Commerce Cloud evolution
  • Skills that can be applied directly in project environments

If you are onboarding new developers or looking to refresh and deepen your SAP Commerce Cloud expertise, these updated Instructor-Led Trainings are a great next step.

entry.title: Re: SAP Build Process automation: Capturing SAP GUI 8.00 Screen fails
entry.content:
Hi @JoachimSiebert 
Thanks for sharing the screenshot — that really helps.

From what I can see, the "Statusinformation" text is part of a grid/table control in the SAP GUI screen. In such cases, it is not possible to directly capture or interact with a single cell as an individual element.

Instead, you need to work with the entire grid.

Here’s what you can try:

  • Select the whole grid (instead of trying to capture the specific text field).
  • Once the grid is identified, use activities like:
    • Get Cell Value (GUI Grid)
    • or similar grid-based actions available in your automation tool

Note:
SAP GUI grids expose data using column keys and row numbers, not as separate UI elements. That’s why the text "User does not exist in remote system" is not captured individually.

You can identify the Column Key by using a Get Rows activity and checking the output structure.

So the correct approach is:
Work with the grid → Read the cell value → Process the text

Regarding the BTP account:

You’re right — with a trial account, updating the Desktop Agent is not possible. There is also no general public BTP account available for this purpose.

  • If your current setup is working fine, you can continue with it

Best Regards
Abirami T

entry.title: Re: Prevent Dynamic Break being applied for Absences - Ti...
entry.content: The client does not have a streamlined work schedule concept in place, so I am using placeholder 8‑hour work schedules. From a time recording perspective, this approach works well because we are not evaluating anything against Planned Working Time. However, an issue arises with dynamic breaks. For example, when a dynamic break is configured to be generated after 4 hours, the system is also applying this break to absence calculations, which is not expected behavior. As a result, when a full‑day absence is requested, the system deducts 7.5 hours instead of 8 hours due to the dynamic break being subtracted. We need a configuration or setting that allows the system to ignore dynamic break generation during absences.

entry.title: Deploying S/4HANA Additional Application Servers on Kubernetes
entry.content:
This blog post is the third part in a series around containerizing SAP S/4HANA systems. I encourage you to read Part 1: Containerizing SAP S/4HANA Systems with Docker and Part 2: Deploying SAP S/4HANA Containers with Kubernetes. In the previous posts, we discussed what Docker is and how we can use it in conjunction with SAP S/4HANA to solve problems related to the N + M landscape. In part 2 we discussed how we can use YAML files to rapidly deploy multiple Kubernetes pods running an image of our S/4HANA system.

Since publishing our first blog post on this topic we have successfully implemented this infrastructure across multiple customer landscapes. Most recently, my colleagues and I wrapped up another project with an innovative customer in the CPG industry, where we successfully deployed additional application servers (AAS) in their QA environment using Kubernetes as a PoC. My colleague Maxim Shmyrev and I have been working hard to make this project successful and we are excited about the potential value it brings to our customers.

What Was the Challenge?

The customer reached out to us and explained that they utilize Infrastructure as Code (IaC) to deploy and manage most of their IT infrastructure. They wanted to rapidly spin up multiple additional application systems for their QA system during periods of high demand.

This is a common scenario for many SAP customers: system usage increases, and Basis teams respond by provisioning additional, costly VMs. They then install the AAS using the Software Provisioning Manager (SWPM) and perform the necessary configurations. Meanwhile, hours pass while the QA backlog continues to grow. By the time the additional capacity is available, project timelines have already been impacted. Once demand increases, additional time must be spent decommissioning these expensive VMs.

Our customer wanted to explore whether deploying their AAS on Azure Kubernetes Services (AKS), instead of provisioning multiple VMs, could provide a more efficient and cost-effective solution.

Our Approach

We created a Docker image of our customer’s additional application server for their QA system using Docker and the Software Provisioning Manager (SWPM. When configuring the YAML files to deploy the pods in AKS we added some additional automation steps at the OS level of the container to ensure a seamless connection to the database and PAS during start-up. During some initial testing we easily deployed 10 additional app servers over two nodes in AKS. Each node has approximately 251 GB of memory allocated and is only using ~35.2 GB (14%) of memory to run these 10 app servers. It should be noted that AKS requires a minimum of two nodes, however, the workload itself could run on a single node if required.

During our cost analysis, we compared the Kubernetes-based deployment against a traditional VM-based approach. A standard E32as v5 virtual machine (32 vCPUs, 256 GiB memory) costs approximately $800 per month. Running 10 additional application servers using traditional VMs would therefore cost approximately $8,000 per month.

During our proof of concept, the total monthly cost for running 10 application servers on Kubernetes was approximately $3,000, including nodes, storage, and supporting infrastructure. The compute nodes themselves accounted for approximately $700 of this total cost.

While this estimate includes additional resources used during the proof of concept, and actual costs may vary depending on configuration and usage, the results indicate potential cost savings in the range of 40–60% when deploying additional application servers on Kubernetes.

The Result

This approach enables our customers to deploy multiple additional application servers within seconds, compared to the best-case scenario of approximately four hours required for manual AAS installation. Furthermore, the additional app servers running in Kubernetes pods can instantly be deleted and the nodes can be scaled down or retired when they are no longer needed. This flexibility allows our customers to remain agile, optimize infrastructure costs, and quickly respond to changing workload demands. If you enjoyed this blog post, please stay tuned for other topics my team members and I will be covering next and be sure to leave a comment below if you have any questions!

  1. What is the recommended enhancement point (BAdI/User Exit) to trigger custom logic after WT confirmation that works for both: /SCWM/TO_CONF and /SCWM/RFUI

  3. Can /SCWM/TO_CONFIRM be safely enhanced for this purpose, or is there a standard alternative?

entry.title: Re: Setting Your SAP TM and SAP EWM Project Up for Success – Join Our Continuous Quality Check Pilot
entry.content:
@martinsimkanin Hi Martin,

Yes, the CQC for transformation assessment is applicable to all implementation projects involving SAP TM and SAP EWM, including those where SAP Business Network for Logistics is implemented on top.

However, it does not delve into design details or specific functionalities for SAP TM/SAP EWM. or other systems that may integrate with them.

The CQC focuses on key topics and concepts relevant to the SAP TM and/or SAP EWM project’s starting Prepare phase. It aims to provide essential information to ensure a proper project implementation (e.g.,project governance, project scope definition, system access, stakeholder enablement, etc).

Hope this information helps!

entry.title: Re: CAP, RAP, or Both: Stop Hiding Behind 'It Depends'
entry.content:
@walteraguerom It's a thoughtful post and you're clearly passionate about the subject. 

I'm not sure if every article included my story on this subject from 2023 and an excellent, fact-based SCN blog post referenced in it but mentioning it here just in case.

It makes for a catchy title and a nice "hook", but personally I find the premise of "completely useless answer" rather... disingenuous. The source you've referenced and the blog post above do provide very clear information what the decisions depend on. This would be "useless" only to someone who doesn't read. This post takes similar information and puts it in the bullet point format. And funny enough, it ends basically with "it depends", just differently stated. ¯\_(ツ)_/¯ What's up with that?

The post makes the point several times that this isn't "ABAP hate" but it's very clearly CAP-biased. It reminds me of a famous monologue from My Cousin Vinny movie: from a certain view point, a brick is just a rectangle. 

The "staffing bias" and "team composition" is a valid point. This is a perennial subject in SAP world, but nevertheless something to keep in mind. I do roll eyes though at the old "stubborn ABAPers are an obstacle to progress" adage because I don't believe it's actually as much of a factor. My guess is it's happening at some organizations more that the others. When dealing with specific clients, I'd just ask to keep in mind that we aren't necessarily looking at the whole brick, so to speak. 

Also appreciate the breakdown of "getting the model wrong". I do believe the "RAP to CAP migration" cost are exaggerated though. (Again, the bias is clearly showing.) CDS is the main part, the differences there aren't big. One could probably convert it using ChatGPT in a minute. Reimplementing logic would be more time-consuming - true. But again - have people not heard of all the magical AI tools? "Weeks" estimate for one service can't be based in reality. (Although, of course, "it depends" - ha!)

"CAP first" - ah, now really showing your true colors. 🙂 I don't believe that only one of the models deserves "presumption of innocence" and the other should bear the burden of the proof. They are different models for different purposes. It's the architect's job to gather all the information and make an informed (and hopefully, unbiased) decision based on many different factors. Completely agree with @fegutieri  too.

One important point the readers should take from this is: beware of your (and your architect's) biases and fallacies. I'd be suspicious of both "RAP first" and 'CAP first" camps. Hire the person who says "it depends". 🙂 

Cheers!

entry.title: SAP Cloud Integration (CI) for SAP IBP HPA integration: A real-world implementation case study
entry.content:
In one of the previous blog post, SAP Cloud Integration was introduced as new integration option for SAP IBP. Subsequent blog posts covered technical topics for creating integration flows based on SAP delivered packages and flows for different integration scenarios. These blog posts in addition to highlighting why organizations should consider SAP CI as part of their IBP integration strategy, covered fundamental set-up and configuration steps for using it for integrating SAP IBP with S/4 Hana Cloud.

In this blog, we explore a real-world example of an organization using Cloud Integration to overcome complex integration challenges for their IBP HPA implementation. We share details of challenges, key architectural decisions, and lessons learned from this complex supply chain planning implementation. This blog highlights a proven integration approach that offers valuable learnings for organizations navigating similar challenges.

Integration challenges: Distributed landscape

The organization in focus like other large manufacturing organizations operates an extensive global supply chain. Their landscape includes:

  • Multiple ERP system distributed globally
  • Large product portfolio with complex supply chain spread across locations using different systems

The key challenge was to integrate data from multiple SAP and Non-SAP systems. The integration approach needs to be flexible enough to support multiple source system integration with SAP IBP.

Key decision: Cloud Integration or Real Time Integration

One of the most critical decisions in any IBP implementation is to choose the right integration tool for IBP.  From release 2508 onwards, RTI and SAP CI are the two integration tools available to integrate SAP IBP HPA with SAP S4 Hana private cloud.

 

The sample customer’s implementation team made an early decision to go with a standalone CI integration approach for IBP. Some of the drivers for this decision were:

  • Cloud Integration works well with all SAP and non-SAP ERP systems, compared to RTI which is restricted only to S/4HANA Private edition
  • Readily available Cloud Integration expertise within the organization
  • Operational planning is carried out in ERP system, ruling out need for Order based planning in IBP
  • Business followed monthly planning cycle, with delta data load planned daily

Additionally, the availability of prepackaged reusable assets for SAP Cloud Integration supports accelerated implementation and reduces time to value significantly. It provides comprehensive connectivity options for diverse landscape, and it being under active development from SAP keeps your integration architecture future ready. Its web-based centralized monitoring and error handling capabilities help in reducing the overall maintenance cost. Because of these inherent advantages of SAP CI, the implementation team decided to go with standalone SAP CI integration architecture.

As real-time data sync capability was not needed, RTI didn’t offer any additional business value in comparison to CI in this scenario. However, depending on your business needs, Operational planning (OBP) in IBP and real time sync capabilities could be essential, in that case CI can be used in conjunction with RTI. As per SAP guidelines, integration architecture can utilize both the integration technologies following way:

  • RTI used for integrating master data and order data from S/4 Hana Private edition
  • SAP Cloud Integration (CI) used for all other integration scenarios such as Key Figures or other non-standard objects.

Following SAP help portal page helps you find right integration tool for your business needs: Finding the Right Integration Tool | SAP Help Portal

Integration design philosophy: keep interfaces “Lean” and “Scalable”

An important contributing factor to the successful implementation of CI for SAP IBP was to keep interfaces simple. The customer's implementation team followed a clear guiding principle that Cloud Integration (CI) should be used only for transporting data from one system to another. It should not be used for deriving any business logic. Cloud Integration (CI) is best used for:

  • Data transportation
  • Data format transformation and technical validation
  • Security handling

Business rules, process logic, and calculations were handled in the source or target system. This approach of preprocessing data in source or target system, made sure CI flows are simple, maintainable and reusable.

Preprocessing of data in source system:

To keep the core and IBP Add-on clean on the S/4 Hana, the implementation team decided to use extended CDS views for data preprocessing in S/4 Hana. Cloud integration flows read data directly from CDS views, avoiding modification of extractors and therefore sticking to clean core best practices.

How it works: Multi layered CDS views were created for preprocessing data in S/4 Hana:

  1. Reuse layer CDS views: Built on IBP add-on staging tables and raw tables (for additional data), this layer provided filtered data. As these CDS view also included data from raw tables, any custom data fields not available in IBP add-on staging tables were captured in these CDS views
  2. Consumption layer CDS views: This is primarily used for renaming S/4 fields to IBP fields. Reuse layer CDS views served as input for these CDS views. CI flows read data directly from these CDS views.

Above approach along with Change Data Capture (CDC) capability for CDS extraction allows delta loads for master data and S/4 Hana execution transaction data.

Reusable integration flows in CI:

SAP CI provides prepackaged reusable integration flows, that can be used to integrate all master data and transaction data types with minimal configuration changes. For the sample customer, data integration is executed by running a single prepackaged reusable CI iflow. One reusable integration flow handles all integration scenarios – all master data and key figures, with the help of flexible parameters. These parameters define:

  • Source CDS view to read in S/4 Hana
  • Target Master data and Key figures in IBP
  • Transfer mode: delta or full load

By sticking to preprocessing within the source system using CDS views and integrating using reusable flow, solution is flexible to accommodate future requirements. Any future business requirements can be handled by extending CDS views in the source system, avoiding any changes in the integration layer.

Integration with non-SAP system:

To integrate non-SAP ERP systems, data from multiple ERP systems is first integrated into a Data warehousing system. In Data warehousing system similar CDS view dependent process is used to preprocess data and then a reusable CI flow is used to pull data into IBP. This unified approach means consistent data structure regardless of the source system type.

Back integration to S4 Hana system:

IBP add-on staging tables enable a fire and forget approach for integrating planning output data from IBP to S/4 Hana. CI delivers data to S/4 IBP add-on tables and S/4 process them independently.  

With this lean integration approach, figure below shows high level data flow between different systems:

 

 Key takeaways: Some of the key learnings from this successful implementation of CI integration are:

  • Evaluating business requirements to select the right integration tool for your implementation. Depending on specific scenarios, CI only integration strategy can be effective.
  • Keep integration lean and focus on data transport only. Push transformation and business logic in source and target systems
  • Availability of prepackaged integration flow helps in accelerated implementation, and results in scalable and low maintenance integration solution

entry.title: How to combine SAP Application Management Services (AMS) with corporate IDP?
entry.content:
Hello everyone,

For BTP applications, SAP recommendeds to use AMS for authorisation in future. For this to be possible, users must exist in the Identity Directory of the SAP Cloud Identity Services.

In an enterprise environment, the situation typically looks like this:

  • Employees are managed in the corporate IDP (e.g. Entra ID).
  • The corporate IDP is connected to the SAP Cloud Identity Service
  • A trust exists between the BTP subaccount and the SAP Cloud Identity Services 

In this standard scenario, users are not created in the Identity Directory, but simply routed through SAP Cloud Identity Services. Consequently, no AMS policies can be assigned.

One option would be to use Identity Federation to move users from the Corporate IDP to the Identity Directory. However, in the case of Entra ID, this sync only runs every 40 minutes. Therefore, this is not really a viable solution.

In the past, shadow users were automatically created in SAP Cloud Identity Services, but this is no longer the case and we cannot find an option to enable it.

What is the intended solution? Can AMS not be used in conjunction with a corporate IDP?

Thank you for your response!

entry.title: Re: CAP, RAP, or Both: Stop Hiding Behind 'It Depends'
entry.content:
Hi @Jelena_Perfiljeva, thank you for such a detailed and spirited critique!

I appreciate the link to your 2023 story—definitely a valuable piece of the puzzle. You’re right; 'it depends' is the ultimate truth in SAP architecture, and my 'useless answer' hook was a bit of a tongue-in-cheek way to address that eternal frustration.

However, I believe there is a world of difference between an 'it depends' that comes with taking a stand and diving into the nitty-gritty details, versus a generic 'it depends' that offers no direction. The latter is what I truly find useless, as it leaves the reader exactly where they started. To build on your closing point: I’d much rather hire the person who says, "It depends, and here is exactly why I’d lean towards X."

Regarding the CAP bias: I’ll take that on the chin. While I strive for objectivity, my recent experiences have definitely leaned that way for specific use cases. I’m leaning into it because I’m firmly convinced it’s where the future of the SAP ecosystem is headed. By highlighting this, I’m encouraging the reader to critically consider this path before making a decision—something that, unfortunately, doesn't always happen in the current market, where choices are often made out of habit rather than strategy.

I love the My Cousin Vinny analogy—it’s a great reminder that we’re often looking at the same brick from different angles. At the end of the day, the goal is to spark the exact kind of professional debate we’re having right here.

Cheers!

entry.title: Re: Downloading Data in Different Formats Using Factory Design Pattern
entry.content:
Did you use ChatGPT or something similar to generate this? The code oddly looks like a first version one would get from a basic prompt, but then I think these days even basic GenAI produces better quality.

It just seems very odd that in one place, it's using cl_gui_frontend_services=>gui_download but then in another, GUI_DOWNLOAD FM. Also, the code for download is repeated; there is no exception handling; the parameter in the report should be a radio-button or at least a dropdown, not a text. That's just the obvious stuff.

As an experiment, I gave this very simple prompt to Gemini: "Using factory pattern, create a global ABAP class that can download files in different formats: TXT, PDF, and XLSX. Data to be downloaded would be a standard internal table."

Here is the exact result I got. It's wild that it decided to use SALV method in XLSX download. 🙂 But I think ABAPXLSX is the best way to go here anyway. Note: this is not an endorsement of the result in any way, just showing what one can get in seconds.
-----------------------------

This is a classic use case for the Factory Design Pattern. By decoupling the calling program from the specific implementation logic (TXT vs. XLSX), you make your code significantly easier to maintain and extend—say, if your boss suddenly decides they want JSON support next week.

In ABAP, we’ll achieve this using an Interface for the behavior, Concrete Classes for the specific formats, and a Factory Class to instantiate them.

1. The Interface: ZIF_FILE_DOWNLOADER

First, define the common "contract" that all downloaders must follow.

ABAP
 
INTERFACE zif_file_downloader PUBLIC.
  METHODS download
    IMPORTING
      it_data     TYPE ANY TABLE
      iv_filename TYPE string.
ENDINTERFACE.

2. Concrete Implementation: TXT

This class handles simple tab-delimited text output.

ABAP
 
CLASS zcl_file_downloader_txt DEFINITION PUBLIC FINAL CREATE PUBLIC.
  PUBLIC SECTION.
    INTERFACES zif_file_downloader.
ENDCLASS.

CLASS zcl_file_downloader_txt IMPLEMENTATION.
  METHOD zif_file_downloader~download.
    cl_gui_frontend_services=>gui_download(
      EXPORTING
        filename                = iv_filename
        filetype                = 'ASC'
        write_field_separator   = 'X'
      CHANGING
        data_tab                = it_data
      EXCEPTIONS
        OTHERS                  = 1 ).
  ENDMETHOD.
ENDCLASS.

3. Concrete Implementation: XLSX

For Excel, we can leverage cl_salv_table to get the binary data easily.

ABAP
 
CLASS zcl_file_downloader_xlsx DEFINITION PUBLIC FINAL CREATE PUBLIC.
  PUBLIC SECTION.
    INTERFACES zif_file_downloader.
ENDCLASS.

CLASS zcl_file_downloader_xlsx IMPLEMENTATION.
  METHOD zif_file_downloader~download.
    DATA: lo_table   TYPE REF TO cl_salv_table,
          lt_binary  TYPE solix_tab,
          lv_length  TYPE i,
          lv_xstring TYPE xstring.

    " Convert internal table to SALV and then to XLSX binary
    cl_salv_table=>factory( IMPORTING r_salv_table = lo_table 
                            CHANGING  t_table      = it_data ).
    
    lv_xstring = lo_table->to_xml( if_salv_bs_xml=>c_type_xlsx ).

    " Convert xstring to binary table for download
    cl_bcs_convert=>xstring_to_solix( EXPORTING iv_xstring = lv_xstring
                                      IMPORTING et_solix   = lt_binary ).

    cl_gui_frontend_services=>gui_download(
      EXPORTING
        filename     = iv_filename
        filetype     = 'BIN'
        bin_filesize = xstrlen( lv_xstring )
      CHANGING
        data_tab     = lt_binary ).
  ENDMETHOD.
ENDCLASS.

4. Concrete Implementation: PDF

PDF conversion from a raw internal table usually requires a transformation or a form. Here is a conceptual implementation using the SALV PDF utility.

ABAP
 
CLASS zcl_file_downloader_pdf DEFINITION PUBLIC FINAL CREATE PUBLIC.
  PUBLIC SECTION.
    INTERFACES zif_file_downloader.
ENDCLASS.

CLASS zcl_file_downloader_pdf IMPLEMENTATION.
  METHOD zif_file_downloader~download.
    " Implementation logic similar to XLSX but using 'if_salv_bs_xml=>c_type_pdf'
    " or a functional module like 'SX_OBJECT_CONVERT_OTF_PDF' if using Spool/Smartforms.
  ENDMETHOD.
ENDCLASS.

5. The Factory Class: ZCL_DOWNLOAD_FACTORY

This is the "brain" that decides which object to create.

ABAP
 
CLASS zcl_download_factory DEFINITION PUBLIC FINAL CREATE PRIVATE.
  PUBLIC SECTION.
    TYPES: ty_format TYPE c LENGTH 4.
    CONSTANTS: co_txt  TYPE ty_format VALUE 'TXT',
               co_xlsx TYPE ty_format VALUE 'XLSX',
               co_pdf  TYPE ty_format VALUE 'PDF'.

    STATIC-METHODS get_downloader
      IMPORTING iv_format            TYPE ty_format
      RETURNING VALUE(ro_downloader) TYPE REF TO zif_file_downloader.
ENDCLASS.

CLASS zcl_download_factory IMPLEMENTATION.
  METHOD get_downloader.
    CASE iv_format.
      WHEN co_txt.
        ro_downloader = NEW zcl_file_downloader_txt( ).
      WHEN co_xlsx.
        ro_downloader = NEW zcl_file_downloader_xlsx( ).
      WHEN co_pdf.
        ro_downloader = NEW zcl_file_downloader_pdf( ).
    ENDCASE.
  ENDMETHOD.
ENDCLASS.

How to use it in your Program

The beauty of this setup is that the caller doesn't need to know how the file is created, only that it will be created.

ABAP
 
DATA(lt_data) = VALUE #( ( name = 'Alice' ) ( name = 'Bob' ) ). " Your internal table

" Get the downloader for XLSX
DATA(lo_downloader) = zcl_download_factory=>get_downloader( zcl_download_factory=>co_xlsx ).

IF lo_downloader IS BOUND.
  lo_downloader->download(
    it_data     = lt_data
    iv_filename = 'C:\Temp\Export.xlsx'
  ).
ENDIF.

Why this rocks:

  • Single Responsibility: Each class does exactly one thing (TXT logic stays in the TXT class).

  • Open/Closed Principle: If you want to add CSV support, you just create a new class and update the Factory. You don't have to touch the existing TXT or XLSX code.

  • Type Safety: Using the interface ensures that the calling program always has access to the download method regardless of the format chosen.

entry.title: AM_ASSET - Archiving for a specific asset number

entry.title: How do I validate a ZIP code using an external API in SAP Sales Cloud v2 when registering a customer
entry.content:
Olá especialistas, como posso realizar a validação de CEP usando uma API externa? Na versão V1, eu conseguia fazer isso via SDK. Eu queria poder inserir o CEP e que a API recuperasse os dados do endereço (cidade, bairro, rua). No entanto, na versão V2, ficou mais complicado. Pensei em criar um hook nas configurações, mas a área de endereço do cliente não possui esse hook. Considere usar um mashup, passando o CEP e encaminhando-o para o BTP, mas nesse ponto, os dados do endereço ainda não foram salvos, então não tenho o ID técnico para tentar salvá-los no banco de dados. Vocês conhecem alguma outra maneira de realizar essa validação? 

 

Obrigado Cleber
